"I know how it will be," his sister had said when he was preparing to migrate to this rural retrea6; "you will bury yourself down there and not speak to a living soul, and your nerves will be worse than ever from moping. I shall just give you letters of introduction to all the people I know there. Some of them, as far as I can remember, were quite nice."Framton wondered whether Mrs. Sappleton, the lady to whom he was presenting one of the letters of introduction came into the nice division.6. retreat: a quiet or peaceful "Do you know many of the people round here?" asked the place away from usual life niece, when she judged that they had had sufficient silent 7. communion: quiet sharing communion7.	of thoughts or feelings"Hardly a soul," said Framton. "My sister was staying here, at the rectory, you know, some four years ago, and she gave me letters of introduction to some of the people here." He made the last statement in a tone of distinct regret."Then you know practically nothing about my aunt?" pursued the self-possessed young lady."Only her name and address," admitted the caller. He was wondering whether Mrs. Sappleton was in the married or widowed state. An undefinable something about the room seemed to suggest masculine habitation."Her great tragedy happened just three years ago," said the child; "that would be since your sister's time.""Her tragedy?" asked Framton; somehow in this restful country spot tragedies seemed out of place."You may wonder why we keep that window wide open on an October afternoon," said the niece, indicating a large French window that opened on to a lawn."It is quite warm for the time of the year," said Framton; "but has that window got anything to do with the tragedy?""Out through that window, three years ago to a day, her husband and her two young brothers went off for their day's shooting. They never came back. In crossing the moor to their favorite snipe-shooting ground they were all three engulfed8 in a treacherous9 piece of bog. It had been that dreadful wet summer, you know, and places that were safe in other years gave way suddenly without warning. Their bodies were never recovered. That was the dreadful part of it." Here the child's voice lost its self-possessed note and became falteringly10 human. "Poor aunt always thinks that they will come back someday, they and the little brown spaniel11 that was lost with them, and walk in at that window just as they used to do. That is why the window is kept open every evening till it is quite dusk. Poor dear aunt, she has often told me how they went out, her husband with his white waterproof coat over his arm, and Ronnie, her youngest brother, singing 'Bertie, why do you bound?' as he always did to tease her, because she said it got on her nerves. engulfed: swallowed up or completely coveredtreacherous: dangerous in a hidden wayfalteringly: in a shaky or uncertain mannerspaniel: a kind of dogDo you know, sometimes on still, quiet evenings like this, I almost get a creepy feeling that they will all walk in through that window--"She broke off with a little shudder. It was a relief to Framton when the aunt bustled into the room with a whirl of apologies for being late in making her appearance."I hope Vera has been amusing you?" she said."She has been very interesting," said Framton."I hope you don't mind the open window," said Mrs. Sappleton briskly12; "my husband and brothers will be home directly from shooting, and they always come in this way. They've been out for snipe in the marshes today, so they'll make a fine mess over my poor carpets. So like you menfolk, isn't it?"She rattled on cheerfully about the shooting and the scarcity of birds, and the prospects for duck in the winter. To Framton it was all purely horrible. He made a desperate but only partially successful effort to turn the talk on to a less ghastly13 topic, he was conscious that his hostess was giving him only a fragment of her attention, and her eyes were constantly straying past him to the open window and visit on this tragic anniversary.complete rest, an absence of mental excitement, and avoidance of anything in the nature of violent physical exercise," announced Framton, who labored under the tolerably widespread delusion that total strangers and chance acquaintances are hungry for the least detail of one's ailments and infirmities14, their cause and cure. "On the matter of diet they are not so much in agreement," he continued."No?" said Mrs. Sappleton, in a voice which only replaced a yawn at the last moment. Then she suddenly brightened into alert attention--but not to what Framton was saying."Here they are at last!" she cried. "Just in time for tea, and don't they look as if they were muddy up to the eyes!"Framton shivered slightly and turned towards the niece with a look intended to convey sympathetic comprehension. The child was staring out through the open window with a dazed15 horror in her eyes. In a chill shock of nameless 13. ghastly: frightening or horriblefear Framton swung round in his seat and looked in 	14. infirmities: weaknesses or illnessesthe same direction.	15. dazed: confusedIn the deepening twilight three figures were walking across the lawn towards the window, they all carried guns under their arms, and one of them was additionally burdened with a white coat hung over his shoulders. A tired brown spaniel kept close at their heels. Noiselessly they neared the house, and then a hoarse16 young voice chanted out of the dusk: "I said, Bertie, why do you bound?"	16. hoarse: rough and harshFramton grabbed wildly at his stick and hat; the hall door, the gravel drive, and the front gate were dimly noted stages in his headlong retreat. A cyclist coming along the road had to run into the hedge to avoid an imminent17 collision. 17. imminent: almost certain to happen very soon"Here we are, my dear," said the bearer of the white mackintosh, coming in through the ?Why does Framton choose to run away instead of stopping to check the truth?window, "fairly muddy, but most of it's dry. Who was that who bolted out as we came up?""A most extraordinary man, a Mr. Nuttel," said Mrs. Sappleton; "could only talk about his illnesses, and dashed off without a word of goodbye or apology when you arrived. One would think he had seen a ghost.""I expect it was the spaniel," said the niece calmly; "he told me he had a horror of dogs. He was once hunted into a cemetery somewhere on the banks of the Ganges by a pack of pariah18 dogs, and had to spend the night in a newly dug grave with the creatures snarling19 and grinning and foaming just above him. Enough to make 	anyone lose 	18. pariah: stray; unwantedtheir nerve."	19. snarling: growling angrilyRomance at short notice was her speciality.The India BookmarkIn the Vikram-Betal stories, the ghost Betal often begins with a believable situation and then reveals an unexpected truth at the end. These stories trained listeners not to accept everything at face value.Learning 	Ask a journalist or media worker: Why is it important to verify Through	information before accepting or sharing it?Interaction/circle of the correct answer in the OMR sheet. Who receives Framton Nuttel at the house?Mrs. Sappleton 	(b) Vera 	(c) Mr. Sappleton 	(d) A servantWhat does Vera say happened to her aunt’s husband and brothers?They moved awayThey went hunting and never returned  They were lost in a stormThey went to warFramton Nuttel had letters of introduction from his ______.friend 	(b) sister 	(c) mother 	(d) doctorWhat does Vera say about Framton’s sudden departure?He is ill 	  	(b) He was called urgently   	(c) He is afraid of dogs 	  	(d) He forgot somethingThe story ends with Vera continuing her habit of making up ______.excuses 	(b) lies 	(c) stories 	(d) jokesArrange the following events in the order(1-6)  CG:2, C:2.2	Critical Thinking	 in which they happened in the chapter.Vera quickly makes up another story to explain Framton’s sudden departure. Framton Nuttel visits Mrs. Sappleton’s house carrying a letter of introduction. Vera invents a tragic story about the men who supposedly died in the marsh. Mrs. Sappleton talks cheerfully about her husband and brothers returning soon  	 through the open window.Vera calmly explains that Mrs. Sappleton’s husband and brothers went out  	 through the window and never returned.When the men actually return, Framton Nuttel panics and rushes out of the house. Find lines from the story that tell you: 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingVera began creating mystery around the open window.Framton Nuttel was nervous and uncomfortable meeting strangers.Vera was skilled at inventing stories on the spot.Framton was badly frightened by what he saw.Mrs. Sappleton suddenly became excited on seeing people outside.Answer the following questions  	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingWhat change came in Framton Nuttel’s feelings during his visit and why?How did Framton Nuttel react while listening to Vera’s story? What does this show about his personality?Why did Framton Nuttel feel uneasy during his conversation with Mrs. Sappleton? “Framton ran out of the house in panic.” Explain the situation leading to this reaction. 5. ‘Romance at short notice was her speciality.’Explain this line with reference to the story.	Put on Your Thinking Caps	 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingVera is a central character in The Open Window. Write a character sketch of Vera in about 100–120 words. Highlight her imagination, opinion about Support your confidence, and storytelling abilities. Do you think her prank on Vera with Framton Nuttel was harmless fun or unfair? Give reasons to support incidents from your opinion.	the story.	Dictionary & Vocabulary Skills	  	/A. Use a dictionary to find the meanings, parts of speech and example sentences for the following words. 	creatures, figures, strangers, restful, horribleDistorted WordCorrect WordYour Sentenceconvinsingconfidancenervosnesscuriostytrecherous	Grammar Lab	  	/Direct and Indirect SpeechWhen we repeat the exact words spoken by a person, we use Direct Speech. 	 Example: Vera said, "The hunters will never return."  	 In the above sentence, ‘said’ is the reporting verb.When we report what someone said without using the exact words, we use Indirect Speech. 	 Example: Vera said that the hunters would never return.RulesRemove the quotation marks.Add that if needed.Change the tense to past tense when the reporting verb is in the past.Change pronouns according to the speaker.Direct SpeechIndirect Speech"I am tired," she said.She said that she was tired."We are waiting," they said.They said that they were waiting."I will come tomorrow," he said.He said that he would come the next day. Complete the table by converting the given sentences into Direct or Indirect Speech, as required.Direct SpeechIndirect SpeechVera said, "I know the truth."Framton said that he was surprised.I said, "They will be back soon."Vera said that she had seen them leave.Check changes in pronouns, tense, and time words while converting sentences.ConnectCommunicate	Listening Skills	 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingListen carefully as your teacher reads the passage. Then tick (3) the conclusion that can be drawn from the passage. 	 Things are always as they seem. 	 First impressions can sometimes be misleading.  	 Every mystery has a supernatural explanation.	Speaking Skills	 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingRead the Dialogue AloudRiya: Did you hear? Someone said the old library is haunted.Kabir: Really? Who told you that?Riya: A student from Class VIII. He said he heard strange noises there yesterday.Kabir: Strange noises do not always mean ghosts. Did anyone check what caused them?Riya: No, but many students are talking about it.Kabir: We should find out the facts before believing the story.Riya: That's true. Maybe the noise came from an animal or a broken window. Kabir: Exactly! It's always better to investigate before jumping to conclusions.Imagine that Riya and Kabir visit the library the next day and discover the real cause of the noise. Work in pairs and continue the conversation.Your dialogue should include: SEL Corner  Social Awareness ῀If you see someone sitting alone and looking worried, what would you prefer to do?Ask if they need help 	 (ii) Inform a teacher or elder nearby	 	 (iii)  Offer a kind word or smile 	 (iv) Observe quietly and stay nearbyWhat do you think makes a person feel safe and comfortable in a new place?Friendly behaviour  (ii) Clear communication   (iii) Respect and politeness (iv) Someone showing concern	 Fun & Creativity 	CG:2, C:2.2	Critical ThinkingVera was very good at creating believable stories. Now it's your turn!Write three short statements about an unusual event:One statement must be completely true.One statement must be partly true but exaggerated.One statement must be completely made up.Scan & LearnScan the QR code to access Ask questions, clear yout the worksheet for this doubts, and learn more with chapter.AI.Read your statements aloud to a partner or the class. The listeners must decide which statement is the Truth, which is the Trick, and which is the Tale.
